text: Today I'm going to show you a series of three videos in which bleeding has been controlled laparoscopically in order to share some techniques that can help avoiding conversion to open.

As you can see in this first video, the bleeding arises from a branch of an iliac vessel while trying to separate an endometriotic nodule from uterocerebral and pelvic side wall to surrounding normal structures.

This kind of scenarios require close cooperation and teamwork between first operator and assistant in order to maintain a constant clean operating field and be able to visualize the different anatomical structures.
Once the bleeding point has been identified, it's important to put pressure like you would do in open surgery.
In this case, Johan Grasper was used.
It's important to identify the different anatomical structures that surround your bleeding area.
In this case, you can see the close proximity of bowel on the medial aspect.
As you can see in this part of the video, we are dissecting different structures away from the bleeding vessel.
The idea is that to isolate and clean the vessel as much as possible in order to then apply either a polio diathermy or vascular clips.
In this part of the video you can see how one of the branches of an iliac vessel causing the bleeding is identified.
Metallic clips are then applied on the bleeding vessel.
You can see how in this kind of scenarios it is important to have a festive system with good laparoscopic skills in order to have a steady camera and an operating field always clean.
A secondary bleeding spot is here identified.
As you can see, once again, the bleeding vessel is separated from the surrounding structures.
In this case, it's separated from the medial endometriotic nodule.
And then, hemostasis is achieved, in this case, once again, with metallic clips that isolate the vessel, and then, finally, with the bipolar diathermy.
The Johan raspa, posed laterally, is protecting the ureter from the hot instrument.
We proceed then with the excision of endometriosis while applying some pressure with a tonsil swab.
When going back to check if any bleeding was ongoing, we recognized that the other end of the vessel was bleeding and we therefore applied diathermy and vascular clips
At the end of the procedure, flow seal was applied on the area.
In this second video that I'm going to share with you, the bleeding arised from a large pre-circle vein.
This lady was having this area explored as the pain was coming from this area and MRI suggests the presence of this lesion.
During the dissection, the large vein starts bleeding and once again, like we did in the previous case, we immediately put pressure on the area.
As you can see here, with Johan, the bipolar, we're putting pressure on the bleeding spot.
As we could identify the bleeding spot, we then applied bipolar diathermy in order to stop the bleeding.
Unfortunately, as you can see, the bleeding was not controlled with simple diathermy.
So we used a different technique.
While the assistant was keeping the areas clean, we grabbed a tonsil swab and we applied pressure on the area in order to control the bleeding.
As you can see now that the operating field is more clear thanks to the tonsil swab that is applying pressure on this pre-sacral vein.
We'll then be able to apply our bipolar diathermy on the areas that are bleeding.
We're moving from cranial to caudal in order to achieve a systematic hemostasis.
At the end of the procedure, flow seal was applied on the area.

In this last video that I'm going to share with you, the bleeding arises from a branch of the iliac vessels during excision of deep infiltrated endometriosis, in particular a nodule involving the lumbar sacral trunk and the sciatic nerve.
As you can see, this black spot on the endometriosis visible in the peritoneum of the pelvic side wall is actually only the tip of a iceberg of a big nodule involving the lumbar sacral trunk.
You can see here the chocolate material coming out from the nodule that is involved in the lumbar sacral trunk.
In this kind of procedures, it's very important to obtain a clean and dry operating field.
As you can see here, the blood vessel is coagulated and then it's cut with cold scissors.
Unfortunately, despite the use of diathermy, the bleeding started from this branch of the iliac vessels.
Once again, it's really important to keep the operating field nice and clean, and here is the important role of a first assistant.
Like we did in the previous cases, we first apply pressure, as you can see here with Johan Graspa and with Maryland.
It's very important in these cases not to apply bipolar diatomy blindly because of the risk of damaging the surrounding structures, like in this case the lumbar sacral trunk.
Therefore, dissection of a bleeding vessel is fundamental.

We applied pressure with a tonsil swab to help with hemostasis and once the vessel has been isolated from the surrounding structures hemostasis is achieved first by using a metallic clip and at the end bipolar diathermy helps to complete the hemostasis.
Once the bleeding is then controlled we then finish and complete the excision of endometriosis.
